text: Poultry farming (broilers) is one of the most popular and profitable micro-enterprises for youth in Uganda. A batch of 100 broiler chicks can be raised in 6-8 weeks. Startup costs include: day-old chicks (UGX 3,500-4,500 each), feeds (starter, grower, finisher), vaccines (Newcastle, Gumboro), a simple poultry house, drinkers and feeders. Total startup for 100 birds: approximately UGX 1,500,000-2,000,000. A mature broiler (2kg) sells for UGX 18,000-25,000 in Kampala markets. Profit margin per bird after all costs: UGX 5,000-8,000. Break-even typically in 2-3 batches (4-6 months).

Layer farming requires more initial capital but provides steady income through egg sales. 100 point-of-lay hens cost approximately UGX 25,000-35,000 each. They start laying at 18-20 weeks and peak at 25-35 weeks. A good hen produces 280-300 eggs per year. A tray of 30 eggs sells for UGX 8,000-12,000 in Kampala. Monthly feed cost for 100 layers: approximately UGX 800,000-1,000,000. Monthly revenue from eggs: approximately UGX 900,000-1,200,000. Profit improves after initial investment is recovered, typically 8-12 months.

Key risks in poultry farming: 1) Disease outbreaks (Newcastle disease can wipe out entire flock in days — vaccination is critical). 2) Feed price fluctuations (maize prices vary seasonally, affecting profitability). 3) Market price drops during festive seasons when supply exceeds demand. 4) Theft — poultry houses need basic security. 5) Power outages affecting brooders for young chicks. Mitigations: vaccinate strictly, buy feed in bulk when cheap, diversify sales channels (hotels, restaurants, not just market), install simple padlock systems, have backup paraffin/charcoal brooders.

Marketing your poultry business in Uganda: 1) Create a WhatsApp Business account — post photos of your healthy birds weekly. 2) Join local poultry farmer WhatsApp groups to find bulk buyers. 3) Visit nearby restaurants, hotels, and schools to offer direct supply at slightly below market price for reliable orders. 4) During festive seasons (Christmas, Easter, Eid), advertise early to get pre-orders. 5) Word of mouth is powerful — give a free chicken to your best customer to earn referrals. 6) Put up a simple signboard at your farm entrance.
